content.value: A queue allows insertion at the rear and removal from the front (First In First Out), modeling a real-world line. Both enqueue and dequeue are O(1) with a proper implementation.
The problem with a simple array queue
If you implement a queue with a plain array and always dequeue from index 0, every dequeue operation requires shifting all remaining elements - O(n). A circular queue fixes this.
Treats the underlying array as a ring: front and rear indices wrap around using modulo arithmetic, so freed space at the front is reused without shifting.

Each element has a priority; dequeue always removes the highest-priority element first, regardless of insertion order. Commonly implemented with a heap (see the Heaps lesson) for O(log n) insert/remove, though a simple sorted array or list also works for small datasets.

Allows insertion and removal from both ends - front and rear. A deque can simulate both a stack and a regular queue, which makes it a flexible building block for algorithms like sliding-window problems.

items: Simple array queue: O(n) dequeue due to shifting (unless circular)
Circular queue: O(1) enqueue/dequeue, fixed capacity
Priority queue: dequeue returns highest priority, typically O(log n) with a heap
Deque: O(1) insert/remove at both ends

content.value: In a circular queue, distinguishing 'full' from 'empty' is the classic gotcha - both can look like front == rear. The (rear+1)%SIZE == front trick (sacrificing one slot) is the standard fix taught in DS courses.

quiz.question: What problem does a circular queue solve compared to a plain array queue?
options: It avoids O(n) shifting on dequeue by wrapping indices with modulo
It removes the need for a rear pointer

quiz.question: A deque allows insertion/removal:
options: At both the front and rear
